parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit injection to confirm execution context, then escalate to data exfiltration or privilege actions if permitted.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can alter query logic, potentially read or modify database data depending on DB permissions.
parsed_findings.proof: Use `?dir=asc;--` (or `desc;--`) to break out of `ORDER BY` and inject SQL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `GET /?sort=name&dir=asc;--` and observe SQL execution errors or injected behavior.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `direction` from `request.args['dir']` is concatenated into raw SQL without validation/parameterization, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Target internal admin/metadata endpoints via SSRF, then use retrieved creds/data to compromise adjacent systems.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can access internal network resources (e.g., cloud metadata) and potentially pivot to further attacks.
parsed_findings.proof: GET /fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/fetch` with `url` pointing to an internal IP/hostname; the server proxies the response back in `res.send(data.data)`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated `req.query.url` is fetched server-side, enabling SSRF to internal hosts/services.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject `../` sequences in `file`, bypass inadequate replacement, reach `fs.readFile` with an escaped path, then receive file contents in the response.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can exfiltrate sensitive files readable by the server process.
parsed_findings.proof: Request `GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd` can make `path.join('/srv/data', f)` resolve outside the intended directory before `fs.readFile` runs.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd` and observe the response body contains the targeted file contents.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.query.file` is only partially sanitized and can traverse directories, enabling arbitrary file reads from `/srv/data`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: User controls `state` cookie -> code base64-decodes it -> `pickle.loads` deserializes -> payload `__reduce__/__setstate__` runs -> code execution occurs.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution, session compromise, data theft, and full system takeover under the app's privileges.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can set the `state` cookie to a base64-encoded pickle payload that executes code during `pickle.loads`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request to `GET /resume` with `Cookie: state=<base64(pickle_payload)>` and observe server-side execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Insecure pickle deserialization of attacker-controlled cookie data can lead to arbitrary code execution.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Object.assign(user, req.body) blindly writes client-controlled fields after a weak/unclear authorization guard, enabling role/status field tampering.
parsed_findings.impact: Privilege escalation and unauthorized access to support/admin-only actions.
parsed_findings.proof: Send PATCH /api/users/<your_id> with JSON like {"isSupport":true,"role":"admin"} and observe the account gains support/admin access.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticate as a normal user; PATCH your own /api/users/:id with protected fields in the JSON body; verify elevated permissions.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Mass assignment in user update lets an authenticated user set protected fields (e.g., isSupport/admin) via req.body.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject via `name` (or via `src` after the allowed prefix) into the f-string, then trigger execution through `os.system`.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote attacker can execute arbitrary shell commands on the server, potentially leading to data theft, persistence, or full compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: POST /backup with {"name":"x;id>/tmp/pwn","src":"/var/data/app"} makes the backend run `tar ... /var/data/app` plus the injected `id`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send POST to `/backup` with JSON `name` containing shell metacharacters (e.g. `;`) and any `src` that passes `startswith('/var/data/')`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `name` and partially-validated `src` are interpolated into a shell command executed via `os.system`, enabling OS command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker-controlled markdown -> unsanitized rendered HTML -> stored in DB -> returned to clients -> browser executes JS.
parsed_findings.impact: Stored XSS could steal sessions, perform actions as victims, or deface content.
parsed_findings.proof: Example `body=""` would be rendered and persisted as `html`, then executed when returned to clients.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/comments` with JSON `{"body":""}` then GET `/comments` and observe `alert(1)` execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted `body` is converted to HTML via `markdown.markdown` and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS if the markdown renderer allows raw HTML.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use negative amount to create/alter balances, then combine with concurrency to amplify net effects across multiple requests.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can arbitrarily increase their own balance and manipulate other users' balances via negative transfers.
parsed_findings.proof: Because amt=-100 makes `src < amt` false, `db.set_balance(current_user.id, src - amt)` increases the sender balance while `db.set_balance(to, ... + amt)` decreases the recipient by 100.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer with JSON like {"to":2,"amount":-100} while sender has at least 1 balance.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler casts user-supplied amount to int and only checks src<amt, so negative values bypass the check and invert the debit/credit logic.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Run parallel transfers to win the race on the balance read, then optionally use negative amounts afterward to further skew balances.
parsed_findings.impact: Concurrent requests can bypass the balance check and cause incorrect balances (overdraw/double-spend).
parsed_findings.proof: Two concurrent requests can both read the same `src` at line 5, both pass `if src < amt` at line 6, and then each subtract at line 8, resulting in an overdraft.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two simultaneous POSTs to /transfer with amounts that individually fit the current src but whose sum exceeds it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Balance validation and updates are non-atomic, enabling race conditions where concurrent requests can overdraw before updates are applied.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use decoded claims to satisfy downstream authorization checks that treat verifyToken’s return as trusted identity.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can impersonate arbitrary users/roles by supplying forged JWTs to the endpoint relying on verifyToken output.
parsed_findings.proof: When jwt.verify throws, the code executes the production branch and returns jwt.decode(t), so attacker-controlled token data is accepted.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set NODE_ENV=production, provide an invalid/forged RS256 JWT in Authorization: Bearer <token>, trigger the catch, and observe that decoded claims are returned.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, verification failures fall back to jwt.decode(t), which does not verify signatures, enabling authentication bypass.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit inverted `if uid` check to reach the DB query path, then harvest the returned `email`/row via the HTTP response.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may bypass intended access control and retrieve user data if any sensitive record exists with `id=0`.
parsed_findings.proof: The route casts `id` to `int` then executes `if uid: return {'error': 'forbidden'}, 403`, so only `uid=0` bypasses the 403.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Login, then request `GET /u?id=0` and observe the handler proceeds to `db.fetch_one` instead of returning 403.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authorization logic appears inverted, returning 403 when `id` is truthy and allowing lookups only when `id=0`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Timing leak -> partial HMAC inference -> offline completion -> bypass signature check -> run `process_event(json.loads(body))`.
parsed_findings.impact: Leaked timing can reduce work to forge valid HMAC signatures, enabling unauthorized webhook processing.
parsed_findings.proof: Attacker measures response times across many attempts to infer partial equality positions.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send repeated requests with varying `X-Sig` values while keeping body constant; statistically correlate timing with match prefix lengths.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Signature verification uses non-constant-time comparison (`got == want`), enabling timing side-channel leaks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Large payload -> expensive `json.loads`/event handling -> resource exhaustion -> service disruption.
parsed_findings.impact: DoS can degrade or crash the webhook service by exhausting memory/CPU during parsing and event handling.
parsed_findings.proof: Very large bodies can force heavy parsing and downstream work when signature check passes (or if comparison is vulnerable in practice).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST large `body` with valid `X-Sig` (or via timing-based bypass) and malformed/huge JSON to stress `json.loads` and `process_event`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No request body size limits or input validation before `json.loads(body)`, allowing CPU/memory DoS via oversized or malformed JSON.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Open redirect -> user is sent to attacker site -> credential theft or further exploit via attacker logic/paths.
parsed_findings.impact: Phishing and token/CSRF chaining by redirecting users to attacker-controlled sites after trusted flows.
parsed_findings.proof: Send `GET /go?next=//evil.com` and the response redirects the browser to `//evil.com` (scheme-relative) instead of staying on-site.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Request `/go?next=//evil.com`; 2) Observe `302` with `Location: //evil.com`; 3) Browser navigates to the attacker domain.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated `next` allows an attacker to trigger an open redirect to an external host via scheme-relative URLs like `//evil.com`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker crafts DOCTYPE with external entity -> parser resolves it -> fromstring builds doc -> findtext returns resolved entity content.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may read local files and/or trigger SSRF-style requests to internal hosts, depending on parser behavior.
parsed_findings.proof: Send XML containing an external entity that references a local file or URL; the entity is resolved into doc.findtext('title').
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /xml/import with body: `<!DOCTYPE r [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]><title>&xxe;</title>` (or an HTTP URL).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: XML is parsed with resolve_entities=True and no_network=False, enabling XXE via external entities during fromstring.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Reach the `if (true)` branch and use any credentials to trigger the success path.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can bypass authentication entirely and gain access as a valid user/session.
parsed_findings.proof: Because the condition is always true, any request that reaches this block will be treated as authenticated regardless of `user`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /login with body {"email":"a@b.com","password":"wrong"} and observe that the handler proceeds as if logged in.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authentication logic uses a constant `if ( true )`, which can cause login to succeed without verifying the user/password.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Craft operator-injected body values so `q` becomes a permissive predicate passed to `db.users.findOne(q)`.
parsed_findings.impact: Can authenticate as any existing user (or bypass checks) by forcing the query predicate to evaluate true.
parsed_findings.proof: If the backing `findOne(q)` treats objects/DMO operators, attacker can supply `email`/`password` as operators to match an unintended user.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send JSON like {"email":{"$ne":null},"password":{"$ne":null}} to make `findOne({email:...,password:...})` match broadly.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User lookup builds a query object directly from `req.body`, enabling NoSQL/ORM query operator injection and auth bypass.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"web","first_file":"01-prerequisites-and-environment.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-287","file":"login.js","line_start":4,"line_end":4,"vuln_claim":"Authentication logic uses a constant `if ( true )`, which can cause login to succeed without verifying the user/password.","proof":"Because the condition is always true, any request that reaches this block will be treated as authenticated regardless of `user`.","repro_steps":"POST /login with body {\"email\":\"a@b.com\",\"password\":\"wrong\"} and observe that the handler proceeds as if logged in.","impact":"Attackers can bypass authentication entirely and gain access as a valid user/session.","confidence":0.78,"chain_steps":"Reach the `if (true)` branch and use any credentials to trigger the success path."},{"cwe":"CWE-89","file":"login.js","line_start":2,"line_end":3,"vuln_claim":"User lookup builds a query object directly from `req.body`, enabling NoSQL/ORM query operator injection and auth bypass.","proof":"If the backing `findOne(q)` treats objects/DMO operators, attacker can supply `email`/`password` as operators to match an unintended user.","repro_steps":"Send JSON like {\"email\":{\"$ne\":null},\"password\":{\"$ne\":null}} to make `findOne({email:...,password:...})` match broadly.","impact":"Can authenticate as any existing user (or bypass checks) by forcing the query predicate to evaluate true.","confidence":0.46,"chain_steps":"Craft operator-injected body values so `q` becomes a permissive predicate passed to `db.users.findOne(q)`."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the overflow to overwrite saved RIP/EIP with a controlled address (or ROP chain) to achieve code execution.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can potentially redirect control flow to attacker-chosen code or crash the program.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying >63 bytes to `gets(buf)` overwrites adjacent stack data including the saved return address.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the binary and input a string longer than 64 bytes (e.g., 100 'A's) when prompted for `name:`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The program uses `gets(buf)` on a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling a stack-based buffer overflow.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Extract access_token then replay it as an Authorization bearer token to take over the session.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can steal the bearer token and impersonate the user against protected APIs until the token expires.
parsed_findings.proof: SessionStore.saveToken() writes token directly with p.edit().putString('access_token', token).apply() to SharedPreferences.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: On a rooted device (or via app data access), read shared_prefs/auth.xml and extract the access_token value.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The access token is stored in plaintext in SharedPreferences via putString, enabling offline theft if device storage is accessed.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"mobile","first_file":"01-baseline-and-setup.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-311","file":"SessionStore.kt","line_start":2,"line_end":5,"vuln_claim":"The access token is stored in plaintext in SharedPreferences via putString, enabling offline theft if device storage is accessed.","proof":"SessionStore.saveToken() writes token directly with p.edit().putString('access_token', token).apply() to SharedPreferences.","repro_steps":"On a rooted device (or via app data access), read shared_prefs/auth.xml and extract the access_token value.","impact":"An attacker can steal the bearer token and impersonate the user against protected APIs until the token expires.","confidence":0.78,"chain_steps":"Extract access_token then replay it as an Authorization bearer token to take over the session."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Hardcoded key -> decrypt captured traffic -> recover plaintext and potentially sensitive data.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can decrypt all past and future encrypted data produced by this function.
parsed_findings.proof: KEY is defined as a constant byte string (`KEY = b'0123456789abcdef'`).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Obtain a copy of the deployed code/container/bundle; read the constant KEY; decrypt captured `iv+ciphertext` with AES-CBC.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is hardcoded in source, enabling offline compromise if the file is leaked or recovered from binaries.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: No MAC -> malleable CBC -> attacker-controlled plaintext differences -> privilege/data manipulation.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can alter decrypted plaintext (e.g., permissions/flags) while bypassing integrity validation.
parsed_findings.proof: Ciphertext is returned as `iv + cipher.encrypt(...)` with CBC but no MAC/AEAD tag.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Take a valid output `iv||ct`; flip bits in `ct` (or preceding blocks) to induce controlled changes in the decrypted plaintext; resend/submit and observe no integrity failure if the application only decrypts.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC encryption is used without authentication/integrity checks, making ciphertext malleable and enabling undetected plaintext tampering.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pass `out=NULL` and `out_sz>0` to bypass the guard, then control `n` via `strnlen` up to `out_sz-1` before the NULL write.
parsed_findings.impact: Denial of service via NULL dereference (process crash).
parsed_findings.proof: Call `copy_name("A", NULL, 10)`: the guard doesn’t return, then `memcpy(out,in,n)` writes to NULL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Compile and run with `char *out=NULL; copy_name("A", out, 10);` to trigger a crash in `memcpy`/`out[n]='\0'`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: NULL pointer check is inverted (`if (out || out_sz==0)`), so `out==NULL` with `out_sz>0` reaches `memcpy`/write and dereferences NULL.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"pwn","first_file":"01-environment-baseline.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":5,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"NULL pointer check is inverted (`if (out || out_sz==0)`), so `out==NULL` with `out_sz>0` reaches `memcpy`/write and dereferences NULL.","proof":"Call `copy_name(\"A\", NULL, 10)`: the guard doesn’t return, then `memcpy(out,in,n)` writes to NULL.","repro_steps":"Compile and run with `char *out=NULL; copy_name(\"A\", out, 10);` to trigger a crash in `memcpy`/`out[n]='\\0'`.","impact":"Denial of service via NULL dereference (process crash).","confidence":0.82,"chain_steps":"Pass `out=NULL` and `out_sz>0` to bypass the guard, then control `n` via `strnlen` up to `out_sz-1` before the NULL write."}]}
